1: God commands Moses to send from the camp all who are leprous, or who have a discharge, or who are ceremonially unclean through contact with the dead — so the camp where God dwells is not defiled. Christ fulfills this paradoxically: he enters the uncleanness (touching lepers, the dead) and comes out clean, purifying rather than being defiled (Matt 8:3; John 11:44).
2: The three categories of exclusion — ṣāraʿat (skin disease), discharge, corpse-contact — represent defilement by disease, bodily brokenness, and death. Christ takes all three categories upon himself: he touches the leper, the bleeding woman touches him, he raises the dead — reversing the flow of defilement, carrying all impurity into his own body on the cross (1 Pet 2:24).
3: The exclusion applies to male and female alike — God's dwelling must remain holy because YHWH dwells in the midst of his people. Christ's sacrifice achieves what the expulsions only signaled: complete cleansing such that God now dwells permanently within his people through the Spirit (1 Cor 6:19; Rev 21:3).
4: Israel obeys, sending the unclean outside the camp as commanded. The camp outside the gate becomes the place of defilement — and it is precisely 'outside the gate' that Jesus suffers in order to sanctify the people through his own blood (Heb 13:12), voluntarily taking the place of the expelled.
5: When a man or woman commits any sin against another, they sin against YHWH. The vertical dimension of all sin — it is ultimately an offense against God — means that only divine forgiveness can truly resolve it. Christ atones not merely for social harms but for every sin as a covenant violation against the holy God (Luke 15:21; Ps 51:4).
6: The framework of māʿal (breach of faith against YHWH) reframes every ethical violation as theological treason. Christ's atoning work is correspondingly vast: he satisfies the full weight of every broken covenant obligation, restoring the relationship at its root rather than patching symptoms (Heb 9:15).
7: Confession of sin plus full restitution (qeren) plus the Levitical fifth — a 120% payment — is required. Christ's atonement is a perfect restitution that exceeds the debt: he not only pays what was owed but restores to the offended party richer than before, giving not just forgiveness but adoption and inheritance (Rom 8:17).
8: If the wronged party is dead with no kin-redeemer, the restitution goes to YHWH via the priest. Christ is the ultimate kinsman-redeemer who stands in when no one else can; as High Priest he alone can present the atoning payment that satisfies divine justice (Heb 7:25; 1 John 2:1–2).
9: Every sacred donation brought to the priest belongs to him — the Levitical principle that offerings for God pass through the mediating priest. Christ as the final mediating priest receives all that is offered to God in his name, presenting it perfected before the Father (1 Pet 2:5, believers offering spiritual sacrifices through Christ).
10: What each man gives to the priest is his own; what is consecrated belongs to the priest. The economy of holy giving — where offerings pass through priestly hands to God — is fulfilled in Christ who receives the sacrifices of his people and presents them holy and blameless to the Father (Col 1:22).
11: God instructs Moses concerning the case of suspected marital unfaithfulness (the sotah procedure). The context of covenant jealousy — YHWH as the jealous husband of Israel — frames this ordinance within the larger typology of God's marriage covenant with his people, which Christ fulfills as the faithful bridegroom (Eph 5:25–27).
12: If a man's wife goes astray and acts treacherously — the language of marital betrayal echoes Israel's covenant infidelity toward YHWH. The prophets develop this into a full bridal metaphor: Israel/the church as wife, God/Christ as husband (Jer 3:1–3; Hos 2:2–20; Rev 19:7).
13: The sin of unfaithfulness in secret, without witnesses — a hidden transgression that only the husband knows about. Christ as the bridegroom-judge sees all hidden sin; his eyes 'are like a flame of fire' (Rev 2:18). Yet he is also the husband who covers the shame of his bride through his blood (Eph 5:26–27).
14: The spirit of jealousy seizes the husband — whether his wife is guilty or not. The qinʾâ (jealousy/zeal) of God for covenant fidelity is fierce and just; it is the same zeal with which Jesus cleanses the temple and pursues his wayward people (John 2:17; Num 25:11).
15: The husband brings his wife to the priest with a barley offering — the poor man's grain, without oil or frankincense, an offering of jealousy that brings iniquity to remembrance. Christ bears the full weight of covenant jealousy: he absorbs the wrath that the guilty deserve, presenting himself as the one who remembers sin in order to remove it (Heb 9:26).
16: The priest brings the woman before YHWH. The sotah procedure is explicitly coram Deo — before the face of God. Christ's atoning work is entirely in the presence of the Father: the Son presents himself and the sinner before the divine tribunal, interceding with his own blood as the testimony (Heb 9:24).
17: The priest takes holy water and dust from the tabernacle floor — a mixture of the sacred and the ordinary, the heavenly water and the earth itself. The incarnation is this mixture: the divine (holy water/Word) taking dust (human flesh) into union; the God-man is the ultimate blending of heaven and earth in one person (John 1:14; Gen 2:7).
18: The priest uncovers the woman's head while holding the bitter waters of the curse. The uncovering signifies vulnerability before divine judgment — the shame that belongs to the guilty. Christ was stripped and exposed before the crowd (Matt 27:28), bearing the shame of covenant-breakers in their place (Heb 12:2).
19: The priest adjures the woman: if you are innocent, these bitter waters will not harm you. The oath of innocence points to Christ who truly was innocent — and yet drank the bitter cup of God's wrath fully and willingly for the guilty (Matt 26:39; Isa 51:17).
20: If the woman has been unfaithful — the conditional that names the guilt. The language anticipates the New Testament's forensic framework: the guilty party must either bear the consequences or have them borne by another. Christ assumes the conditional guilt of his people, standing in as the condemned so they go free (2 Cor 5:21).
21: The priest invokes the curse oath: may YHWH make you a curse and an oath among your people, with thigh falling away and belly swollen. The covenant curse here reaches its ultimate expression in Christ who 'became a curse for us' on the cross (Gal 3:13), bearing the full embodied consequence of covenant infidelity in his crucified body.
22: The woman's amen-amen response to the curse seals her self-commitment to the ordeal's justice. Christ's own 'amen' responses throughout his ministry signify his full commitment to the truth of God's word — including the truth that curse falls on the sin-bearer (Matt 26:42).
23: The priest writes the curses and washes them into the bitter water — the ink of the written oath dissolved into the water the woman must drink. Paul's language of the debt-record (cheirographon) nailed to the cross echoes this: Christ dissolves the written indictment against us, absorbing the ink of condemnation into his own death (Col 2:14).
24: The bitter water — water of cursing — is made to enter the woman so it brings bitter pain if guilty. The cup of God's wrath that Christ drinks in Gethsemane and on Calvary is this very cup: the distilled bitterness of divine judgment against covenant-breaking, poured into the innocent one's cup (Matt 26:39; Rev 14:10).
25: The priest waves the grain offering before YHWH and brings it to the altar. The grain offering alongside the bitter water creates a two-element ceremony — the ordinary (grain) alongside the extraordinary (bitter water). Christ's offering likewise joins his ordinary humanity (bread-like grain) to his extraordinary sacrifice (blood-and-curse-bearing) in one complete act.
26: The priest takes a handful of the grain as memorial portion and burns it on the altar; then makes the woman drink the water. The memorial offering (ʾazkārâ) is a token of the whole — just as Christ's Supper is the memorial of his whole sacrifice, a portion taken as the remembrance of the complete redemptive act (Luke 22:19).
27: If the woman is guilty, the water becomes bitter within her, bringing curse and public shame. If innocent, she is unharmed and will bear children. The twofold outcome — condemnation for the guilty, vindication and fruitfulness for the innocent — mirrors the eschatological judgment Christ brings: condemnation for the impenitent, life and fruitfulness for those united to him (John 3:18–19; John 15:5).
28: The innocent woman is declared clean and shall conceive children. Fruitfulness follows vindication — the innocent wife's restored fertility reflects the church's fruitfulness after Christ's vindication and resurrection. The union between Christ and his church bears the fruit of new birth in the Spirit (John 15:16; Gal 4:26).
29: This is the law of jealousy — the full instruction for when the spirit of jealousy comes over a husband. The conclusion underscores that God is the covenant husband who authored this procedure; Christ as the jealous bridegroom who cleanses his church 'by the washing of water with the word' (Eph 5:26) is the antitype of this ordinance.
30: The principle applies whether the jealousy was justified or not — the ritual works either way, clearing the innocent and condemning the guilty. Christ's atonement covers all: he receives the guilty who flee to him and vindicates the innocent who rest in his righteousness, with the outcome perfectly corresponding to reality as God sees it (Rom 8:1).
31: The man is free from iniquity; the woman bears her own iniquity. The conclusion of the sotah law separates guilt properly — no innocent party bears what belongs to another. Yet Christ voluntarily assumes what does not belong to him, so that his people can be declared free with his own freedom. He bears iniquity that is not his; they receive innocence that is not theirs — the great exchange (2 Cor 5:21; Isa 53:11).
